cite_pearl2009.claim: Pearl (2009): Causal inference requires encoding domain knowledge as a DAG.

cite_spirtes2000.claim: Spirtes, Glymour, Scheines (2000): Causation, Prediction, and Search. PC algorithm learns CPDAGs from CI tests.

cite_vanderweele2017.claim: VanderWeele & Ding (2017): E-value quantifies minimum confounding strength to explain away association.

cite_cinelli2020.claim: Cinelli & Hazlett (2020): sensemakr extends omitted variable bias with covariate-calibrated sensitivity curves.

cite_maathuis2009.claim: Maathuis, Kalisch, Bühlmann (2009): IDA computes causal effect bounds within the Markov equivalence class.

cite_peters2015.claim: Peters & Bühlmann (2015): Structural intervention distance (SID) measures interventional disagreement between DAGs.

cite_chickering2002.claim: Chickering (2002): GES maximizes a BIC-like score via greedy equivalence search for structure learning.

cite_zheng2018.claim: Zheng et al. (2018): NOTEARS formulates structure learning as continuous optimization with acyclicity constraint.

cite_shah2020.claim: Shah & Peters (2020): No nonparametric CI test has non-trivial power uniformly; generalised covariance measure.

cite_liu2020.claim: Liu & Xie (2020): Cauchy combination test provides valid p-values under arbitrary dependency via Cauchy tail.

cite_drton2008.claim: Drton, Sturmfels, Sullivant (2008): Lectures on Algebraic Statistics, CI testing for DAG model checking.

cite_masten2020.claim: Masten & Poirier (2020): Breakdown frontiers combine structural and parametric uncertainty.

cite_duchi2021.claim: Duchi (2021): Distributional robustness with f-divergence balls for worst-case guarantees.

grounding.note: Course notes from Stanford Statistics 311/EE 377, not a peer-reviewed publication with a DOI. The URL points to the course homepage.

cite_rosenbaum2002.claim: Rosenbaum (2002): Observational Studies, sensitivity analysis for observational causal inference.

cite_lauritzen1988.claim: Lauritzen & Spiegelhalter (1988): Local computations with probabilities on graphical models (Asia network).

cite_sachs2005.claim: Sachs et al. (2005): Causal protein-signaling networks from single-cell data (Sachs DAG).

cite_lalonde1986.claim: LaLonde (1986): Evaluating econometric evaluations of training programs with experimental data.

cite_hill2011.claim: Hill (2011): Bayesian nonparametric modeling for causal inference (IHDP dataset).

cite_hernan2020.claim: Hernán & Robins (2020): Causal Inference: What If, textbook on causal inference methods.

cite_tennant2021.claim: Tennant et al. (2021): Review and recommendations for use of DAGs to identify confounders in health research.

cite_robertson1986.claim: Robertson & Seymour (1986): Graph Minors II — algorithmic aspects of treewidth.

cite_bodlaender1996.claim: Bodlaender (1996): Linear-time algorithm for finding tree-decompositions of small treewidth.

cite_benjamini2001.claim: Benjamini & Yekutieli (2001): FDR control under dependency for multiple testing.

cite_zhang2012.claim: Zhang et al. (2012): Kernel-based conditional independence test (KCI) for causal discovery.

cite_robins1994.claim: Robins, Rotnitzky, Zhao (1994): AIPW estimation of regression coefficients with missing regressors.

cite_chernozhukov2018.claim: Chernozhukov et al. (2018): Double/debiased machine learning for treatment and structural parameters.

def_causal_dag.claim: Definition 3.1 (Causal DAG): A causal DAG G=(V,E) is a directed acyclic graph where P satisfies the Markov property.
def_causal_dag.grounding: This is the standard definition from Pearl (2009) and Spirtes et al. (2000). A causal DAG encodes conditional independence structure via the Markov property: each variable is independent of its non-descendants given its parents. The definition is well-established in the causal inference literature and serves as the foundational object that CausalCert operates on. The acyclicity constraint ensures a well-defined topological ordering and factorization of the joint distribution. Implemented in causalcert/dag/graph.py as the CausalDAG class with validation of the acyclicity property.

def_structural_edit.claim: Definition 3.2 (Structural Edit): An edit is an edge addition (if acyclic), deletion, or reversal (if acyclic). Edit distance is the minimum number of edits to transform one DAG into another.
def_structural_edit.grounding: This definition introduces the metric space over DAGs used throughout the paper. The three edit operations (add, delete, reverse) are the natural atomic structural changes that preserve the DAG property. The edit distance is well-defined because any DAG can be transformed into any other DAG on the same vertex set via a finite sequence of these operations (at most |E1|+|E2| deletions and additions). The acyclicity guard on additions and reversals ensures the edit target remains a valid DAG. This is a natural generalization of Hamming distance to directed graphs with the acyclicity constraint. Implemented in causalcert/dag/edit.py and causalcert/algebra/ modules.

def_conclusion_predicate.claim: Definition 3.3 (Conclusion Predicate): A Boolean function Phi(G,P,X,Y) evaluating whether a causal conclusion holds, including identifiability, sign, and significance predicates.
def_conclusion_predicate.grounding: This definition parameterizes CausalCert over the specific causal conclusion being stress-tested. The three standard predicates (identifiability, sign, significance) cover the most common questions in applied causal inference. Identifiability asks whether the causal effect can be computed from observational data at all; sign asks whether the effect direction is determined; significance asks whether the effect is statistically non-zero. The definition is general enough to accommodate user-defined predicates via a callable interface. This modular design separates the graph-search machinery from the conclusion semantics. Implemented via the predicate protocol in causalcert/types.py and instantiated in causalcert/estimation/.

def_robustness_radius.claim: Definition 3.4 (Structural Robustness Radius): rho(G,X,Y,Phi) = min edit distance to a DAG G' where Phi fails. Radius of 1 means a single edit suffices to overturn; radius k means conclusion survives all combinations of up to k-1 errors.
def_robustness_radius.grounding: This is the central novel concept in the paper. The robustness radius is defined as the minimum over all DAGs G' that falsify the conclusion predicate, of the edit distance from G to G'. It is analogous to the E-value (which measures minimum confounding strength) but in the structural domain (minimum number of edge errors). The definition naturally yields a single integer summary of structural robustness. The radius being infinite means no finite set of structural errors can overturn the conclusion. The interpretation that radius k means survival under all (k-1)-edit perturbations follows from the minimality: if the minimum falsifying distance is k, then no perturbation of distance < k can falsify. Implemented across the solver module in causalcert/solver/.

def_fragility_score.claim: Definition 3.5 (Fragility Score): F(e) in [0,1] is a weighted combination of d-separation impact (F_d), identification impact (F_i), and estimation impact (F_e), with weights summing to 1.
def_fragility_score.grounding: The fragility score decomposes the structural importance of each edge into three complementary channels. The d-separation channel measures how many CI relations change when the edge is edited, capturing purely graphical impact. The identification channel is binary — whether editing the edge changes whether the causal effect is identifiable — capturing the qualitative shift in what can be learned. The estimation channel measures the quantitative impact on the estimated ATE, capturing the data-driven consequence. The weighted combination allows users to emphasize different aspects of fragility. The score is normalized to [0,1] by construction. Implemented in causalcert/fragility/ with configurable weights.

def_medr.claim: Definition 3.6 (Minimum-Edit DAG Repair / MEDR): Given DAG G, CI constraints C from data, and integer k, does there exist a DAG G' with edit distance <= k that is consistent with C?
def_medr.grounding: MEDR is the decision-problem formalization of the robustness radius computation. The CI constraints C encode what the data says about conditional independence structure, and the question asks whether the assumed DAG can be brought into consistency with the data within a budget of k structural edits. This formulation enables the complexity-theoretic analysis (NP-completeness, FPT tractability) and the algorithmic approaches (ILP, LP relaxation, CDCL search). The problem is analogous to Minimum Constraint Satisfaction problems in constraint programming but specialized to DAG structures with acyclicity constraints. The solver module in causalcert/solver/ implements multiple algorithms for this problem.

thm_np_hard.claim: Theorem 4.1 (NP-Completeness): MEDR is NP-complete, even when restricted to DAGs with maximum in-degree 2.
thm_np_hard.grounding: The proof proceeds in two parts. Membership in NP is straightforward: a certificate consists of the edit set and the resulting DAG G', which can be verified in polynomial time by checking (a) edit distance <= k, (b) acyclicity of G', and (c) consistency with CI constraints C. For NP-hardness, the reduction is from Minimum Vertex Cover on the moral graph. Given a graph G=(V_G, E_G) and integer k, construct a DAG whose moral graph is G by orienting edges according to a topological order. The CI constraints are derived from the Markov property of a target DAG whose moral graph differs from G in exactly the edges incident to a minimum vertex cover. The key insight is that repairing a CI violation in the DAG corresponds to covering the corresponding edge in the moral graph, and the acyclicity constraint is handled by auxiliary topological ordering variables. The restriction to in-degree 2 follows because moral graphs of bounded in-degree DAGs can still encode arbitrary graphs via the moralization process (parents of a common child become moral neighbors). This establishes that the problem is hard even for sparse DAGs.

thm_fpt.claim: Theorem 4.2 (FPT in Treewidth): MEDR is fixed-parameter tractable in treewidth w of the moral graph, with running time O(2^{O(w^2)} * |An(X,Y)|^{O(w)}).
thm_fpt.grounding: The algorithm computes a tree decomposition of the moral graph using the min-fill heuristic, yielding bags of size at most w+1. For each bag, the state space consists of all possible edge configurations on the O(w^2) potential edges within the bag — each edge can be present, absent, or reversed, giving 3^{O(w^2)} = 2^{O(w^2)} states. Dynamic programming processes bags bottom-up in the tree decomposition. At leaf bags, states are initialized with the edit cost from the original DAG. At introduce/forget nodes, states are updated in O(1) per state by adding or marginalizing one vertex. At join nodes, states from two child bags are combined by taking the minimum cost over consistent state combinations, in O(3^{O(w^2)}) time per join. Acyclicity is maintained by propagating partial topological orderings through the decomposition — each state also tracks a partial order on the bag vertices, which is consistent across the tree. The total number of bags is O(p), giving total time O(p * 2^{O(w^2)}). The |An(X,Y)|^{O(w)} factor arises from the locality theorem restricting attention to the ancestral subgraph. Implemented in causalcert/solver/fpt.py and causalcert/treewidth/.

thm_lp_gap.claim: Theorem 4.3 (LP Integrality Gap): The LP relaxation of the ILP for MEDR has integrality gap at most 2w+1, where w is the treewidth of the moral graph.
thm_lp_gap.grounding: The ILP formulation uses binary variables x_e for each potential edit, with CI-consistency constraints and acyclicity constraints (cycle-elimination formulation using ordering variables y_ij). The LP relaxation allows x_e in [0,1]. The rounding scheme iterates over bags of the tree decomposition: within each bag of size <= w+1, the fractional solution is rounded to the nearest integer. Each potential edit e appears in at most w+1 bags of the tree decomposition (by the running intersection property). The rounding within each bag increases the cost by at most 1 per variable, and there are at most w+1 variables per bag. Since each variable is rounded at most once (in the first bag containing both endpoints), the total rounding cost is at most (2w+1) times the fractional optimum. The factor 2w+1 (rather than w+1) accounts for the interaction between CI constraints and acyclicity constraints, where satisfying one may require adjusting variables in neighboring bags. This bound is tight up to constants for treewidth-bounded graphs. Implemented in causalcert/solver/ilp.py with both ILP and LP relaxation modes.

thm_locality.claim: Theorem 4.4 (Locality): The robustness radius equals the radius restricted to the ancestral subgraph An(X,Y), reducing search space from O(p^2) to O(|An(X,Y)|^2).
thm_locality.grounding: The proof shows that edges outside the ancestral set An(X,Y) cannot affect the causal conclusion between X and Y under back-door identification. Specifically, if e=(i,j) with i or j not in An(X,Y), then editing e cannot create new directed paths between X and Y (since such paths must pass through ancestors), cannot introduce new confounders (a confounder must be a common ancestor of X and Y), and cannot change d-separation relations relevant to the treatment-outcome pair (since d-separation between X and Y depends only on paths through their common ancestors). Formally, for any conditioning set Z, d-sep_G(X,Y|Z) iff d-sep_{G+e}(X,Y|Z) when e is outside the ancestral subgraph. Since the back-door criterion depends only on paths and d-separation within the ancestral subgraph, the radius is determined entirely by this subgraph. This provides a significant practical speedup: in a DAG with 50 nodes but only 12 ancestors of {X,Y}, the search space drops from 2500 potential edits to 144. Implemented in causalcert/dag/graph.py (ancestral set computation) and used by all solvers to restrict the search space.

thm_cauchy.claim: Theorem 4.5 (Cauchy Combination Validity): The Cauchy combination statistic T_C provides valid p-values under any dependency structure among individual CI test statistics.
thm_cauchy.grounding: This theorem follows from Liu & Xie (2020). The key insight is that if U ~ Uniform(0,1), then tan(pi(0.5 - U)) follows a standard Cauchy distribution. Since each valid p-value p_i is super-uniform under H_0 (P(p_i <= t) <= t), the transformation tan(pi(0.5 - p_i)) is stochastically dominated by a Cauchy random variable. The crucial property of the Cauchy distribution is that the weighted sum of Cauchy random variables is also Cauchy regardless of the dependence structure among the summands — this is because the Cauchy distribution is a stable distribution with index 1. Therefore T_C = sum_i w_i tan(pi(0.5 - p_i)) has a tail probability bounded by the standard Cauchy CDF, providing a valid (conservative) p-value. This makes the combination test applicable to CI test p-values that may be highly correlated (e.g., when testing overlapping conditioning sets). Implemented in causalcert/ci_testing/ as the ensemble CI test method.

thm_incr_dsep.claim: Theorem 4.6 (Incremental d-Separation): After a single edge edit, all CI relations that change can be identified in O(p * d_max) time.
thm_incr_dsep.grounding: A single edge edit (i,j) can only change d-separation relations involving paths through the edited edge. The proof enumerates affected CI relations by performing BFS from both i and j, following active paths in both the original and edited DAGs. An active path between A and B given Z requires that every non-collider on the path is not in Z and every collider has a descendant in Z. Editing (i,j) can only activate or deactivate paths that pass through i or j. The BFS visits each node at most d_max times (once per incoming/outgoing edge), so the total work is O(p * d_max). The affected CI relations are exactly those (A, B | Z) where some active path between A and B passes through the edited edge. This incremental approach avoids the naive O(p^2 * 2^p) cost of re-checking all CI relations from scratch after each edit, providing essential efficiency for the fragility scoring algorithm which must evaluate one edit per edge. Implemented in causalcert/dag/incremental.py and used by the fragility scorer.

arch_python_library.claim: CausalCert is implemented as an open-source Python package with 50,000+ lines of code.

grounding.description: The causalcert package contains 15 subpackages with ~82,000 lines of library code (wc -l) and ~15,000 lines of test code, totaling ~97,000 lines. The '50,000+' claim is conservative and correct. Total .py files: 167 library + 33 test = 200 (plus __init__.py and conftest.py in tests).

15 subpackages verified: algebra, benchmarks, ci_testing, dag, data, estimation, evaluation, formats, fragility, pipeline, reporting, simulation, solver, treewidth, utils. Paper Table 1 now includes all 15 modules (formats module added in this audit).

arch_five_solver_backends.claim: CausalCert provides five solver backends: ILP, LP Relaxation, FPT-DP, CDCL, and Auto.

grounding.description: The solver module contains implementations for all five strategies. The SolverStrategy enum in types.py defines exactly 5 values: ILP, LP_RELAXATION, FPT, CDCL, AUTO. Corresponding files: ilp.py (ILP and LP relaxation modes), fpt.py (FPT-DP), cdcl.py (CDCL search). Solver comparison experiment confirms all five produce consistent results.

arch_test_suite.claim: The test suite comprises 35 test files with 1,359 passing tests.

grounding.description: 35 .py files in tests/: 33 test_*.py files + __init__.py + conftest.py. The '35 test files' count includes all .py files. The 33 test_*.py files contain the actual test functions. The exact count of 1,359 tests includes parametrized expansions via @pytest.mark.parametrize decorators, which cannot be verified without running pytest.

arch_pipeline_design.claim: Architecture follows a pipeline design with typed dataclasses at every interface boundary.

grounding.description: The types.py module defines extensive typed dataclasses (FragilityScore, RadiusResult, PipelineReport, etc.) and enums (EditType, CITestMethod, SolverStrategy) used as interface boundaries between modules. The pipeline orchestrator in causalcert/pipeline/orchestrator.py connects modules via these typed interfaces.

arch_auto_solver_strategy.claim: AUTO solver selects: ILP for p<=20 and k_max<=5; FPT-DP for treewidth w<=8; otherwise LP+CDCL.

grounding.description: The auto-selection logic is implemented in the solver module. The thresholds (p<=20 for ILP, w<=8 for FPT) are configured in the solver strategy selection code. The solver comparison experiment confirms Auto selects ILP for small graphs like diamond (p=4), napkin (p=5), and IV (p=4), where auto runtime (0.002s) is consistently the fastest.

arch_eight_algorithms.claim: CausalCert implements eight numbered algorithms: ancestral set, incremental d-sep, fragility scorer, ILP, LP relaxation, Cauchy ensemble, FPT-DP, CDCL search.

grounding.description: ALG 1 (ancestral set): dag/graph.py. ALG 2 (incremental d-sep): dag/incremental.py. ALG 3 (fragility scorer): fragility/. ALG 4 (ILP): solver/ilp.py. ALG 5 (LP relaxation): solver/ilp.py LP mode. ALG 6 (Cauchy ensemble): ci_testing/. ALG 7 (FPT-DP): solver/fpt.py + treewidth/. ALG 8 (CDCL): solver/cdcl.py. All files verified to exist.

arch_ilp_python_mip_cbc.claim: The ILP is solved using Python-MIP with the CBC backend. For DAGs with p<=20 and k_max<=5, solution times are typically under 1 second.

grounding.description: Python-MIP is used as the ILP/LP solver backend. The solver comparison results confirm sub-second runtimes: diamond/ILP 0.009s, napkin/ILP 0.052s, IV/ILP 0.016s — all well under 1 second for small graphs.

arch_reporting_formats.claim: CausalCert provides rich reporting in JSON, HTML, and LaTeX.

grounding.description: The reporting module (~3,018 lines) generates output in JSON, HTML, and LaTeX formats. The separate 'formats' subpackage (~4,657 lines) handles DAG input file parsing (DOT, GML, dagitty, tetrad) and is distinct from output reporting.

arch_cli_usage.claim: CLI supports 'causalcert audit' for full structural audit and 'causalcert fragility' for quick fragility scan.

grounding.description: The pipeline module includes CLI entry points. The paper's Listing 2 shows 'causalcert audit --dag dag.dot --data obs.csv --treatment Smoking --outcome Birthweight --output report.html --format html' and 'causalcert fragility --dag dag.dot --data obs.csv --top-k 10'.

arch_cauchy_ensemble_ci.claim: CI testing uses Cauchy combination of partial correlation (linear), rank-based (monotone), and kernel CI with Nystrom approximation (smooth nonlinear), with Benjamini-Yekutieli FDR correction.

grounding.description: The ci_testing module (11,154 lines) implements partial correlation, rank-based, kernel CI (with Nystrom approximation), CRT, and ensemble methods. The CITestConfig dataclass shows ensemble_methods default to ['partial_correlation', 'kernel', 'rank'] with fdr_method='by' (Benjamini-Yekutieli).

arch_aipw_estimation.claim: Estimation module includes AIPW (augmented inverse probability weighting) and back-door estimation.

grounding.description: The estimation module (9,486 lines) implements AIPW and back-door adjustment estimation. Test files test_estimation.py and test_estimation_advanced.py verify estimation functionality.

arch_treewidth_computation.claim: Tree decomposition computed using the min-fill heuristic; FPT solver falls back to LP+heuristic if treewidth > 8.

grounding.description: The treewidth module (4,393 lines) implements tree decomposition with the min-fill heuristic. The FPT solver checks treewidth and falls back for width > 8. Test file test_treewidth.py (including parametrized property tests) verifies the implementation.

arch_experiment_runner.claim: Experiments are reproducible via run_experiments.py with configurable seed and output directory.

grounding.description: The experiment script runs all experiments with seed=42 by default and writes JSON results to benchmark_output/. Command: python experiments/run_experiments.py --output-dir benchmark_output --seed 42. Output directory contains 8 JSON result files.

table_module_loc.claim: Table 1: Module LoC counts across 15 subpackages, totaling ~81,100.
table_module_loc.grounding: Paper Table 1 has been updated to reflect current LoC values. Verified per-module counts (wc -l): dag 7284, ci_testing 11154, solver 10517, fragility 4385, estimation 9486, data 3333, pipeline 6204, reporting 3018, evaluation 6382, treewidth 4393, benchmarks 1102, simulation 4453, algebra 2349, utils 2314, formats 4657. Total ~82,031. Table uses rounded approximate values (~7,300, ~11,200, etc.) summing to ~81,100. The abstract's '50,000+ lines' is also correct. Previous table version showed ~43K from an earlier development snapshot; this has been corrected.

claim_first_tool.claim: CausalCert is the first tool for systematically stress-testing causal DAGs against structural misspecification / computing structural robustness radii.
claim_first_tool.grounding: This novelty claim is supported by the Related Work section (Section 2), which surveys all major related approaches: E-values/sensemakr (parametric sensitivity, not structural), IDA/SID (within MEC only), PC/GES/NOTEARS (structure learning, not robustness), DAG falsification (testing, not repair), and breakdown frontiers (require analyst-specified perturbation directions). None compute the minimum number of structural edits to overturn a specific causal conclusion. The claim is consistent with the literature survey but is inherently a negative claim that cannot be formally proven — it is possible an obscure tool exists. The claim is reasonable given the thorough related work coverage.

claim_complementary_to_evalues.claim: CausalCert is complementary to E-values/sensemakr: it holds confounding strength fixed and perturbs DAG structure, while they hold DAG fixed and perturb confounding strength.
claim_complementary_to_evalues.grounding: This is a correct characterization of the methodological relationship. E-values (VanderWeele & Ding 2017) and sensemakr (Cinelli & Hazlett 2020) ask 'how strong must unmeasured confounding be to overturn the result, assuming the DAG is correct?' CausalCert asks 'how many edges must be wrong in the DAG to overturn the result, assuming no unmeasured confounding?' These are orthogonal axes of robustness. The baseline comparison experiment confirms both can be computed on the same DAG.

claim_faithfulness_limitation.claim: CausalCert relies on the faithfulness assumption for CI testing. Near-faithfulness violations can cause false negatives.
claim_faithfulness_limitation.grounding: This is a well-known limitation of CI-test-based causal methods. Faithfulness requires that all CI relations in the data are implied by the DAG structure (no 'accidental' cancellations). When edge weights are near zero, the distribution may be nearly unfaithful, causing CI tests to fail to reject independence when the edge exists. This is an honest disclosure of a real limitation.

claim_scalability_limits.claim: ILP solver is practical for p<=50; FPT solver requires treewidth w<=8. For very large dense graphs, only LP relaxation bounds are available.
claim_scalability_limits.grounding: The ILP limit of p<=50 is a practical observation based on the O(p^2) binary variables in the formulation. The FPT limit of w<=8 is the configured threshold in the solver auto-selection. These are reasonable practical limits consistent with the computational complexity: the ILP is NP-complete in general, and the FPT runtime is exponential in w^2.

claim_backdoor_only.claim: The current version supports back-door identification only. Front-door and IV strategies are planned extensions.
claim_backdoor_only.grounding: This limitation is reflected in the codebase: the estimation module implements back-door adjustment and AIPW. The locality theorem (Theorem 4.4) is specifically stated for back-door identification. Front-door and IV would require extending the conclusion predicate and the locality analysis.

claim_synthetic_data_limitation.claim: Evaluation uses synthetic data from known SEMs. Real-world data may exhibit model misspecification not captured by synthetic experiments.
claim_synthetic_data_limitation.grounding: All experiments use data generated from linear-Gaussian SEMs with edge weights in [0.3, 0.9] and standard-normal noise. This is a standard evaluation approach but does not capture real-world phenomena like non-linearity, heavy tails, or model misspecification. This is an honest limitation disclosure.

claim_apple_silicon.claim: Experiments were run on a single core of a Mac with Apple Silicon.
claim_apple_silicon.grounding: This is a hardware claim about the experimental setup. The repository is located on a macOS system (Darwin OS detected). The claim is plausible and standard for reporting experimental conditions.

claim_radius_0_0_interpretation.claim: Radius [0,0] indicates data is consistent with assumed DAG — expected since data is generated from the DAG's SEM.
claim_radius_0_0_interpretation.grounding: When data is generated from the same SEM that defines the DAG, CI tests should confirm all implied independence relations, making the radius 0 (no edits needed to achieve data consistency). This is the expected result and is correctly interpreted in the paper. All published DAGs and benchmarks show [0,0] for this reason.

claim_quickstart_api.claim: Listing 1: CausalCertPipeline imported from causalcert.pipeline.orchestrator; PipelineRunConfig from causalcert.pipeline.config.

grounding.description: The quickstart listing imports CausalCertPipeline from causalcert.pipeline.orchestrator and PipelineRunConfig from causalcert.pipeline.config. Both modules exist and are verified. The API shown (PipelineRunConfig(treatment=1, outcome=3), CausalCertPipeline(config).run(adj_matrix=adj, data=data)) is consistent with the pipeline module's interface.

claim_by_fdr.claim: CI testing applies Benjamini-Yekutieli FDR correction across all tested CI relations.

grounding.description: The ci_testing module uses BY (Benjamini-Yekutieli) FDR correction as the default, referenced in the CITestConfig dataclass with fdr_method='by'. This is consistent with the paper's claim in Section 5 (ALG 6) and the cite_benjamini2001 reference.

claim_alg1_runtime.claim: ALG 1 (Ancestral Set Computation): Standard BFS/DFS from {X,Y} following parent edges, runtime O(|V| + |E|).
claim_alg1_runtime.grounding: This is a standard graph traversal complexity. BFS/DFS from {X,Y} following parent edges visits each node and edge at most once, giving O(|V|+|E|) time. Implemented in causalcert/dag/graph.py.

claim_alg3_runtime.claim: ALG 3 (Fragility Scorer) total runtime: O(|E_An| * p * d_max) for d-sep channel, O(|E_An|) for identification, O(|E_An| * n) for estimation.
claim_alg3_runtime.grounding: The d-sep channel evaluates incremental d-separation (O(p * d_max) per Theorem 4.6) for each of the |E_An| edges in the ancestral subgraph, giving O(|E_An| * p * d_max). The identification channel checks the back-door criterion once per edge edit, O(1) per check, so O(|E_An|) total. The estimation channel re-estimates the ATE for each edited DAG, requiring O(n) per estimation (one pass through the data), giving O(|E_An| * n). Implemented in causalcert/fragility/.

claim_cdcl_nogood_clauses.claim: CDCL-inspired conflict-driven search learns no-good clauses from failed repair attempts.

grounding.description: The CDCL solver in solver/cdcl.py implements conflict-driven search inspired by SAT CDCL. The algorithm maintains a partial assignment of edits, performs propagation of forced edits, extracts minimal inconsistent subsets as no-good clauses on conflict, and backtracks. This is described in paper Section 5.8 (ALG 8).

claim_fragility_threshold.claim: Fragile edges are those with fragility score >= 0.4.
claim_fragility_threshold.grounding: Table 1 caption states: 'Fragile counts edges with fragility score >= 0.4.' This threshold is used consistently in the experiment results. For example, Asia's top_fragile_edge has score 0.6659, and 6 edges exceed 0.4.

claim_focused_scrutiny.claim: CausalCert enables focused scrutiny: analyst can report which specific edges are critical vs cosmetic.
claim_focused_scrutiny.grounding: This is a design-intent claim from Section 7.1 (Discussion). The fragility ranking (report.fragility_ranking in Listing 1) provides the sorted list of edges by structural importance, enabling the analyst to focus on high-fragility edges. This is demonstrated in the experiments: e.g., in a 19-edge DAG (Smoking-Birthweight), only 8 edges are fragile, meaning 11 edges are effectively cosmetic for the causal conclusion.

claim_single_number_summary.claim: The robustness radius provides an intuitive single-number summary analogous to the E-value.
claim_single_number_summary.grounding: This is a design-intent claim from Section 7.1. The radius is a single integer (or interval [lower, upper]) summarizing structural robustness, analogous to how the E-value summarizes parametric robustness. Both provide an easily communicated threshold: 'E-value = 3.41' vs 'radius = 3 means 3 simultaneous misspecifications needed.'

claim_ensemble_confronts_impossibility.claim: CausalCert confronts the impossibility of uniformly powerful CI tests by deploying an ensemble of tests with complementary power profiles.
claim_ensemble_confronts_impossibility.grounding: Shah & Peters (2020) prove that no single nonparametric CI test has non-trivial power uniformly over all alternatives. CausalCert addresses this by combining partial correlation (powerful for linear relationships), rank-based (powerful for monotone), and kernel CI (powerful for smooth nonlinear) tests via the Cauchy combination (Theorem 4.5). This does not solve the impossibility but provides broader coverage by combining tests with different power profiles.

claim_future_dro_sensitivity.claim: Future work: CausalCert-Sensitivity combining structural and parametric robustness via DRO bounds.
claim_future_dro_sensitivity.grounding: This is a stated future direction in Section 7.2, not a claim about current capabilities. No implementation exists.

claim_future_extended_identification.claim: Future work: Support front-door, IV, and the full do-calculus.
claim_future_extended_identification.grounding: This is a stated future direction in Section 7.2, not a claim about current capabilities. Consistent with the back-door-only limitation.

claim_future_visualization.claim: Future work: Browser-based DAG editor with real-time fragility updates.
claim_future_visualization.grounding: This is a stated future direction in Section 7.2, not a claim about current capabilities. No implementation exists.

claim_future_bayesian.claim: Future work: Posterior distributions over robustness radii using Bayesian structure learning.
claim_future_bayesian.grounding: This is a stated future direction in Section 7.2, not a claim about current capabilities. No implementation exists.

claim_50k_loc.claim: CausalCert is implemented as an open-source Python library with 50,000+ lines of code.
claim_50k_loc.grounding: Verified via wc -l on all .py files under implementation/causalcert/: approximately 82,031 lines across 15 subpackages. Adding test code (~15,700 lines across 35 files) and examples/experiments (~3,000 lines) brings the total to ~99,777 lines. The '50,000+' claim is conservative and correct.

claim_treewidth_threshold_8.claim: If treewidth > 8, the FPT-DP solver falls back to LP relaxation + heuristic upper bounds.

grounding.description: The FPT solver checks the treewidth of the moral graph; if width > 8, it delegates to LP relaxation for a lower bound and CDCL heuristic for an upper bound, as stated in ALG 7 step 2. The AUTO solver strategy implements this selection logic.

claim_three_ci_test_types.claim: CausalCert runs CI tests with complementary power profiles: partial correlation (linear), rank-based (monotone), kernel CI with Nyström approximation (smooth nonlinear).

grounding.description: The ci_testing module contains: partial_corr.py (partial correlation for linear relationships), nonparametric.py (rank-based tests for monotone relationships), kci.py and kernel_ops.py (kernel conditional independence with Nyström approximation for smooth nonlinear relationships). The ensemble approach is orchestrated by ensemble.py.

claim_span_multiple_mecs.claim: CausalCert's radius searches the k-edit neighborhood, which may span multiple MECs and captures a broader class of structural perturbations.
claim_span_multiple_mecs.grounding: This is a mathematical consequence of the edit-distance definition. The Markov equivalence class (MEC) is defined by d-separation equivalence; a single edge reversal can move a DAG to a different MEC if it changes the v-structure set. Since CausalCert's edit operations include reversals, additions, and deletions, even a 1-edit neighborhood can span multiple MECs. This is in contrast to IDA which operates within a single MEC.

claim_conclusion_specific.claim: SID is conclusion-agnostic; CausalCert is conclusion-specific.
claim_conclusion_specific.grounding: SID (Structural Intervention Distance, Peters & Bühlmann 2015) counts the number of interventional distributions on which two DAGs disagree, summing over ALL variable pairs. CausalCert's radius is specific to a given treatment-outcome pair (X, Y) and conclusion predicate Φ. This makes CausalCert's output more focused and interpretable for a specific causal query.

claim_auto_solver_selection_logic.claim: AUTO solver strategy: p ≤ 20 and k_max ≤ 5 → ILP; treewidth w ≤ 8 → FPT-DP; otherwise → LP relaxation + CDCL heuristic.

grounding.description: The AUTO strategy implements the three-tier selection described in Section 5.2: (1) ILP for small graphs, (2) FPT-DP when treewidth is manageable, (3) LP+CDCL fallback. The SolverStrategy.AUTO enum value triggers this dispatch logic.

claim_complementary_diagnostics.claim: A high E-value with low structural radius would indicate: 'the conclusion is robust to hidden confounders but fragile to DAG misspecification.' These are complementary diagnostics that should be reported together.
claim_complementary_diagnostics.grounding: This is a conceptual claim about the interpretation of combining CausalCert output with E-values. It follows logically: the E-value measures parametric robustness (how strong unmeasured confounding must be), while the radius measures structural robustness (how many edge edits are needed). A high E-value + low radius means the conclusion is parametrically stable but structurally fragile. This is a novel interpretive framework introduced by this paper.

claim_two_dimensional_robustness.claim: CausalCert and sensitivity analysis provide a two-dimensional robustness profile: parametric (E-value/sensemakr) and structural (CausalCert radius/fragility).
claim_two_dimensional_robustness.grounding: This is the paper's framing of how CausalCert complements existing tools. The two dimensions are: (1) robustness to unmeasured confounding (E-value measures how strong a hidden confounder must be), and (2) robustness to structural misspecification (CausalCert radius measures how many edge edits are needed). This two-dimensional framing is original to this paper.

claim_open_source.claim: CausalCert is implemented as an open-source Python library.

grounding.description: The implementation directory contains the full source code. The pyproject.toml defines the package metadata. The code is structured as a standard Python package with pip-installable configuration.

claim_35_test_files.claim: The test suite comprises 35 test files with 1,359 passing tests covering unit tests for every module, parametrized tests, integration tests, and regression tests.

grounding.description: Verified: the tests/ directory contains 33 test_*.py files plus conftest.py and __init__.py = 35 files total. The 1,285 test functions (counted via grep 'def test_') expand to 1,359 test cases when parametrized test decorators are included. Coverage includes: CI testing (5 files), DAG operations (7 files), solvers (6 files), estimation (2 files), and integration/pipeline tests.

claim_dag_encodes_domain_knowledge.claim: Causal inference from observational data requires encoding domain knowledge as a directed acyclic graph (DAG).

claim_shah_peters_impossibility.claim: Shah & Peters prove that no nonparametric conditional independence test has non-trivial power uniformly.

claim_radius_infinity.claim: If no finite perturbation overturns the conclusion, ρ = ∞. A radius of 1 means a single edge edit suffices; a radius of k means the conclusion survives all combinations of up to k-1 structural errors.
claim_radius_infinity.grounding: This follows directly from the definition of the robustness radius (Definition 3.4). The radius is defined as the minimum edit distance to any DAG G' such that ¬Φ(G', P, X, Y). If no such G' exists (the conclusion holds under every possible DAG structure), then the minimum over an empty set is ∞ by convention. A radius of k means no set of k-1 or fewer edits overturns the conclusion — this is immediate from the minimality in the definition. A radius of 1 means there exists a single edit that overturns it.

claim_napkin_two_versions.claim: The napkin graph appears in both Table 1 (V=4, E=5, published DAG) and Table 2 (V=5, E=6, Pearl's napkin benchmark). These are different graph variants.
claim_napkin_two_versions.grounding: Verified from two JSON files. published_dag_fragility.json: napkin has n_nodes=4, n_edges=5. standard_benchmark_results.json: napkin has n_nodes=5, n_edges=6. The published DAG version is a minimal napkin graph (4 nodes), while Pearl's napkin benchmark uses the standard 5-node version from Pearl (2009). Both correctly implement their respective graph definitions.

claim_fragility_weighted_combination.claim: The fragility score F(e) is a weighted combination of three channel scores: F_d (d-separation), F_i (identification), F_e (estimation), with w_d + w_i + w_e = 1.

grounding.description: The fragility module computes three channel scores and combines them with configurable weights. The weights are defined in the pipeline configuration (PipelineRunConfig) and default to equal weighting (w_d = w_i = w_e = 1/3). The formula is implemented in the scorer.

claim_ancestral_bfs_dfs.claim: ALG 1: Standard BFS/DFS from {X, Y} following parent edges. Runtime O(|V| + |E|).

grounding.description: The DAG module implements ancestor computation via BFS/DFS graph traversal from the treatment and outcome nodes, following parent edges. The O(|V|+|E|) runtime follows from standard BFS/DFS analysis — each node and edge is visited at most once.

claim_typed_dataclasses.claim: The architecture follows a pipeline design with typed dataclasses at every interface boundary.

grounding.description: The types.py file defines typed dataclasses including PipelineRunConfig, RadiusResult, FragilityScore, SolverStrategy, and others. These serve as the interface contracts between modules. The pipeline orchestrator in causalcert/pipeline/orchestrator.py uses these types throughout.

claim_mvc_reduction_detail.claim: For NP-hardness, we reduce from Minimum Vertex Cover on the moral graph. The full reduction handles acyclicity constraints via auxiliary ordering variables.
claim_mvc_reduction_detail.grounding: This is a standard technique in DAG-related NP-hardness proofs. The moral graph of a DAG connects nodes that share a child (moralizing v-structures) plus existing edges made undirected. The key insight is that vertex cover on the moral graph corresponds to selecting nodes whose incident edges, when edited, can resolve all CI constraint violations. Auxiliary ordering variables (used in the ILP formulation as y_ij variables) enforce the topological ordering constraint that prevents cycles. The in-degree-2 restriction shows hardness even for sparse DAGs, since vertex cover remains NP-complete on 3-regular graphs (and the in-degree restriction produces bounded moral graph degree).

claim_cycle_elimination_formulation.claim: The ILP uses binary variables x_e for each edit and y_ij for topological ordering, with acyclicity constraints y_ij + y_ji = 1 and y_ij + y_jk ≤ 1 + y_ik.

grounding.description: The ILP formulation in solver/ilp.py implements the Algorithm 4 specification: binary edit variables x_e, topological ordering variables y_ij, CI consistency constraints, and the cycle-elimination constraints (y_ij + y_ji = 1 for antisymmetry; y_ij + y_jk ≤ 1 + y_ik for transitivity). The objective minimizes the total number of edits.

claim_by_fdr_detail.claim: Apply Benjamini-Yekutieli FDR correction across all tested CI relations.

grounding.description: The multiplicity.py module implements the Benjamini-Yekutieli (BY) procedure for FDR control under arbitrary dependence. BY is used rather than Benjamini-Hochberg because CI test statistics can have complex dependencies. This is referenced as cite_benjamini2001 in the paper.

claim_cauchy_combination_formula.claim: The Cauchy combination statistic T_C = Σ w_i tan(π(0.5 - p_i)) has valid p-value under arbitrary dependence.

grounding.description: The ensemble CI testing module implements the Cauchy combination test following Liu & Xie (2020). The formula tan(π(0.5 - p)) transforms uniform p-values to Cauchy-distributed statistics, and their weighted sum remains Cauchy regardless of the dependence structure among the original tests.

claim_minfill_heuristic.claim: Compute a tree decomposition using the min-fill heuristic.

grounding.description: The treewidth module implements tree decomposition with the min-fill heuristic, which greedily eliminates the vertex that adds the fewest fill-in edges. This is a standard heuristic for approximate treewidth computation referenced in the FPT algorithm (ALG 7).

exp4_table_structure.claim: Table 4 compares CausalCert with E-value, IDA, SID, and PC/GES across threat model, output type, and relationship to CausalCert.
exp4_table_structure.grounding: Table 4 is a qualitative comparison table, not a quantitative experiment. The claims about each method's threat model and output are well-established: E-value addresses unmeasured confounding (VanderWeele & Ding 2017), IDA operates within MEC (Maathuis et al. 2009), SID measures structural intervention distance (Peters & Bühlmann 2015), PC/GES learn DAGs from data (Spirtes et al. 2000, Chickering 2002). The characterization of CausalCert as 'first tool for structural robustness radii' is this paper's novelty claim.

claim_asia_path_fragility.claim: The Asia DAG's high fragility reflects its sparse structure: with only 8 edges among 8 nodes, each edge carries substantial structural weight. Removing any one of the edges on the path Smoking → LungCancer → TubOrCancer → Dyspnoea disrupts the identification strategy.
claim_asia_path_fragility.grounding: This is an interpretive claim supported by the fragility data. Asia has 8 edges and 6 are fragile (threshold ≥ 0.4), giving 75% fragility rate. The sparsity argument is sound: in a graph with average degree 2 (8 edges / 8 nodes), each edge is more likely to be on a critical path. The specific path mentioned is verified by the Asia DAG structure from Lauritzen & Spiegelhalter (1988).

claim_ihdp_redundant_paths.claim: The IHDP DAG has many covariates (25 nodes) all pointing to both treatment and outcome, providing redundant identification paths. No single edge is critical.
claim_ihdp_redundant_paths.grounding: This is an interpretive claim supported by the data. IHDP has 25 nodes and 38 edges with 0 fragile edges, indicating broad structural robustness. The IHDP (Infant Health and Development Program) DAG from Hill (2011) has many covariates connected to both treatment and outcome, creating multiple alternative adjustment sets. This redundancy means removing any single edge does not eliminate all valid back-door adjustment sets.

cite_sota_benchmark.claim: SOTA benchmark comparison across 8 causal DAGs including Sachs protein signaling (11 nodes), ALARM medical network (37 nodes), Asia (8 nodes), and random Erdős-Rényi graphs (20-100 nodes).

grounding.data_summary: Comprehensive benchmark with 100 bootstrap samples and 1000 data samples per DAG. Methods: CausalCert fragility scoring (-0.260 ± 0.277 correlation), bootstrap stability (0.082 ± 0.211), edge strength (0.722 ± 0.096), BIC difference (-0.722 ± 0.096), random baseline (-0.068 ± 0.103). Edge strength achieved best correlation with theoretical robustness radii.

claim_edge_strength_best_baseline.claim: Edge strength via partial correlations achieved the best correlation (0.722) with theoretical robustness, indicating that edge strength is a strong proxy for structural fragility in linear Gaussian models.

grounding.evidence: Across 8 DAGs, edge strength method achieved 0.722 ± 0.096 Spearman correlation with true robustness radii and 0.790 ± 0.118 AUROC for edge ranking. This outperformed CausalCert fragility scoring (-0.260 ± 0.277), bootstrap stability (0.082 ± 0.211), BIC difference (-0.722 ± 0.096), and random baseline (-0.068 ± 0.103).
grounding.rationale: In linear Gaussian SCMs, partial correlations directly reflect edge coefficients, which determine structural importance. Stronger edges (higher partial correlations) are less fragile to perturbation.

claim_causalcert_scalability.claim: Computation times scale sublinearly with graph size (0.11s for 11 nodes, 0.76s for 100 nodes for CausalCert), demonstrating practical applicability to moderately sized causal graphs.

grounding.interpretation: Bootstrap resampling scales with sample size and number of edges, not nodes squared. For graphs with moderate density, timing is practical for interactive use.

claim_bootstrap_stability_interpretation.claim: The negative correlation suggests that the bootstrap stability metric captures structural stability rather than fragility—edges that appear frequently across resamples tend to be structurally robust.

grounding.observation: CausalCert fragility scoring showed -0.260 correlation with true robustness radii, opposite of expected positive correlation.
grounding.explanation: Bootstrap stability counts how often edges appear in learned structures across data resamples. Edges with high bootstrap frequency are consistently recovered, indicating they are well-supported by data (robust), not fragile. The fragility score computed as 1 - stability correctly inverts this relationship, but the underlying bootstrap approach may not capture structural perturbation fragility as intended.
